unit.name: Self-supervised learning recap and scalable contrastive variants
unit.description: Reviews the previous lecture's self-supervised learning setup and quickly situates MoCo, DINO, and DINOv2 before starting generative models.
unit.summary: The lecture opens by revisiting self-supervised learning as unlabeled pretraining: an encoder-decoder is trained on a pretext task without human labels, then the decoder can be discarded and the learned representation transferred to smaller labeled tasks [ts=41s] [ts=71s]. It recalls image-based pretext tasks such as rotation, rearrangement, and reconstruction, then reframes the later part of the recap around contrastive learning, where different augmentations of the same image are pulled together while augmentations from different images are pushed apart [ts=130s] [ts=190s]. The instructor then summarizes why SimCLR needs large batches, explains MoCo's workaround of keeping a queue of earlier keys plus a momentum encoder updated as an exponential moving average of the main encoder, and notes that DINO replaces the loss with a KL-based objective [ts=324s] [ts=466s] [ts=537s]. The recap ends by emphasizing DINOv2 as a practically important self-supervised feature extractor whose main advance was scaling training from ImageNet-sized data to roughly 142 million images, yielding strong reusable visual features [ts=568s] [ts=598s].

key_points.text: Self-supervised learning uses pretext tasks on unlabeled data, then transfers the encoder to downstream labeled tasks.
Contrastive learning pulls together augmentations from the same image and pushes apart augmentations from different images.
MoCo keeps a queue of previous keys and updates a momentum encoder as an exponential moving average of the main encoder weights.
DINO replaces the SimCLR-style loss with a KL-divergence-based objective.
DINOv2 scaled training to about 142 million images and is highlighted as a strong practical source of image features.
